one_line_takeaway: By restricting the membership-inference signal to positions where a fine-tuned LLM predicts incorrectly and measuring the directional (up vs. down) imbalance of probability shifts against a pretrained reference, EZ-MIA achieves up to 8x higher TPR@0.1%FPR than the prior state-of-the-art (SPV-MIA) using only two forward passes and no auxiliary model training.
key_finding: On WikiText with GPT-2 (full fine-tuning), EZ-MIA reaches AUC 0.984 vs. SPV-MIA's 0.899, with TPR@1%FPR of 66.3% vs. SPV-MIA's 17.5% (3.8x) and TPR@0.1%FPR of 14.0% vs. 1.75% (8.0x). On AG News with Llama-2-7B (LoRA), TPR@1%FPR is 46.7% vs. SPV-MIA's 15.8% (3x). Averaged over the 9 dataset/model configurations in Table 1, EZ-MIA improves AUC by +0.25 over LOSS, +0.25 over Zlib, +0.28 over Min-K%++, and +0.06 over SPV-MIA (0.84 average AUC for SPV-MIA, the strongest prior baseline). Fully fine-tuned GPT-2 results: AUC 0.984 (WikiText), 0.950 (AG News), 0.993 (XSum); TPR@1%FPR 66.3%/39.4%/82.6% respectively. LoRA-tuned models (GPT-J, Llama-2) average 0.86 AUC vs. 0.98 for full fine-tuning. A separate controlled comparison (Table 2, XSum) isolating fine-tuning method from model scale shows TPR@1%FPR drops 55x on GPT-2 (82.6% to 1.5%), 13x on GPT-2-XL (98.6% to 7.7%), and 6x on Llama-2 (99.0% to 17.6%) when moving from full fine-tuning to LoRA, establishing fine-tuning method (not model scale) as the primary determinant of measured privacy risk. Domain generalization: 0.893 AUC / 38.8% TPR@1%FPR on Swallow-Code with Stable-Code-3B. Mechanistic validation (Table 3): rank-improvement gaps between members and non-members are 4-6x larger at error positions (0.087-0.173) than at success positions (0.014-0.043), all p < 0.0001 (Mann-Whitney U), supporting the paper's structural explanation. Robustness check across perplexity-based difficulty quartiles on XSum/GPT-2-XL shows AUC range of only 0.046 (0.878-0.924), i.e., no systematic degradation on harder samples. A named failure mode: false negatives on XSum/GPT-2-XL are longer (430 vs. 354 tokens average) with more error positions (230 vs. 173) and negative mean delta, while false positives are shorter (94 vs. 363 tokens) with fewer errors and unusually high mean delta from the reference model performing poorly.
core_contribution: The paper introduces EZ-MIA, a training-free membership inference attack (MIA) against fine-tuned autoregressive language models. Its central novel idea is that memorization signal concentrates at 'error positions' -- tokens where the fine-tuned model's top prediction is wrong -- rather than being spread evenly across all tokens as prior aggregate-score attacks assume. At each error position the attack computes the token-level log-probability difference between the fine-tuned target model and the original pretrained (pre-fine-tuning) checkpoint used as a reference. It sums the positive deltas (P, upward-moved probability mass) and the magnitude of negative deltas (N, downward-moved mass) over the error set, and defines the Error Zone score EZ(x) = P/N, a scale-invariant statistic (invariant to uniform rescaling of the deltas) that a threshold classifies as member/non-member. The attack needs only two forward passes per query (target + reference) and requires no shadow models, no reference-model fine-tuning, and no classifier training -- an explicit efficiency contrast with SPV-MIA (~42 forward passes plus reference fine-tuning) and the Neighborhood Attack (~101 forward passes).
threat_model: Adversary has (1) query access to the target model theta sufficient to obtain token-level log-probabilities for arbitrary input sequences, and (2) access to the pretrained base checkpoint from which the target was fine-tuned (or a public model of comparable architecture) as the reference model. No access to samples from the target's actual training distribution and no ability to train auxiliary/shadow models is assumed. The paper states this matches realistic attack scenarios against fine-tuned open-weight models and APIs that expose token probabilities. The EZ score computation additionally assumes access to ground-truth tokens of the queried sequence (needed to define the error set E), which the paper's own Limitations section flags as restricting applicability to settings like membership inference and training-data extraction (where the attacker supplies candidate sequences) rather than to privacy attacks lacking ground truth.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: (1) EZ-MIA is not universally superior -- on WikiText with Llama-2-7B, SPV-MIA achieves marginally higher AUC (0.780 vs. 0.771); (2) evaluation is scoped entirely to the fine-tuning setting -- applying EZ-MIA to models pretrained on web-scale data is explicitly called an open challenge because memorization signal is weaker/more diffuse there (citing Duan et al. 2024's finding that MIAs against large pretrained models are often ineffective); (3) LoRA experiments use only a single hyperparameter configuration (rank 16, alpha 32), so the relationship between LoRA rank/alpha and measured leakage is unexplored; (4) the method requires ground-truth tokens to define the error set, which holds for membership inference/data extraction settings but may not generalize to other privacy-attack settings without ground truth. Additional grounded caveat: SPV-MIA and the Neighborhood Attack were both reimplemented/run by the authors themselves (SPV-MIA via official code, Algorithm 2 variant) under the paper's own experimental protocol rather than taken from the original papers' reported numbers, which the authors frame as controlling for confounds but which also means baseline numbers are the authors' own reproduction.

quantitative_results: WikiText/GPT-2 (full FT): EZ-MIA AUC 0.984, TPR@1%FPR 66.3%, TPR@0.1%FPR 14.0%
AG News/GPT-2 (full FT): EZ-MIA AUC 0.950, TPR@1%FPR 39.4%, TPR@0.1%FPR 9.8%
XSum/GPT-2 (full FT): EZ-MIA AUC 0.993, TPR@1%FPR 82.6%, TPR@0.1%FPR 33.1%
AG News/Llama-2-7B (LoRA): EZ-MIA TPR@1%FPR 46.7% vs SPV-MIA 15.8% (3x)
Average AUC across 9 configs: EZ-MIA outperforms SPV-MIA by +0.06 (SPV-MIA average 0.84)
Table 6 average TPR@0.1%FPR: EZ-MIA 10.1% vs SPV-MIA 1.76% (5.7x average multiplier)
Fine-tuning method effect (XSum, TPR@1%FPR, Table 2): GPT-2 124M full 82.6% vs LoRA 1.5% (55x); GPT-2-XL 1.5B full 98.6% vs LoRA 7.7% (13x); Llama-2 7B full 99.0% vs LoRA 17.6% (6x)
Robustness to difficulty (XSum/GPT-2-XL quartiles by reference perplexity): AUC Q1 0.924, Q2 0.893, Q3 0.878, Q4 0.887 (range 0.046)
Rank-improvement gap at error vs. success positions (Table 3): 0.087-0.173 at error positions vs 0.014-0.043 at success positions (4-6x larger), all p<0.0001
Training-epoch dynamics (XSum/GPT-2-XL): AUC 0.895 after epoch 1 rising to 0.957 after epoch 3; TPR@1%FPR 54.1% (epoch 1) to 90.2% (epoch 3)
One exception where EZ-MIA underperforms: WikiText/Llama-2-7B AUC 0.771 (EZ-MIA) vs 0.780 (SPV-MIA)
baselines_compared: Reference Loss / RefL (Carlini et al. 2022 framework)
